species.name: Morels
lookForOutside: Honeycombed caps at ash, elm, or apple edges
Hollow stem when sliced — never brain-like false morels
Cool mornings after rain on north slopes below 1,200 ft

investigation.where: Old orchard edge or river terrace below 1,200 ft — public land only
observe: Any honeycombed fungi — photograph, do not pick uncertain specimens
doNotDisturb: Do not rake leaf litter or dig soil
Leave small specimens for spore dispersal

scoreNarratives.high: Season and moisture still align on low terraces and orchard edges — the window is narrow but real.
scoreNarratives.moderate: Some zones remain worth a quiet walk; heat and drying slopes are ending the flush.
scoreNarratives.low: Season has passed for most elevations — wait for next spring or shift species.

species.name: Chanterelles
lookForOutside: No fruiting yet — study oak litter depth and moss on north slopes
Gill-like ridges, not true gills; peppery apricot smell when mature
Sustained June rain on oak ridges is the signal to return

investigation.where: North-facing oak slope, 1,000–1,400 ft — map a stand before mushrooms appear
observe: Any orange fungi — likely too early; verify ID
writeDown: Would this slope hold moisture after two dry days?
doNotDisturb: Do not harvest immature or uncertain fungi

scoreNarratives.high: Moisture and oak habitat align — fruiting may be beginning on favored slopes.
scoreNarratives.moderate: Too early for harvest, not too early to learn habitat — north oak slopes after rain are the classroom.
scoreNarratives.low: Season has not opened — use this week to scout oak ridges for a return visit in late June.

species.name: Black Trumpets
lookForOutside: Not in season — note hemlock ravines and mossy draws for a late-summer return
Black, vase-shaped fungi on forest floor near decaying wood
Often hidden in leaf litter — patience, not scraping

investigation.where: Hemlock ravine with moss and decaying logs — scout now, harvest months away
writeDown: Why might trumpets favor this draw?
doNotDisturb: Do not peel bark or roll logs extensively

scoreNarratives.high: Late-season moisture and hemlock habitat may support fruiting — verify on the ground.
scoreNarratives.moderate: Season approaching — scout ravines now; return after sustained late-summer rain.
scoreNarratives.low: Months until peak — use this time to map hemlock draws and study forest moisture.

species.name: Ramps
lookForOutside: Broad smooth leaves with onion smell when crushed — two leaves per bulb
Past peak in most Pike County sites — leaves may be yellowing
If harvesting: take one leaf per patch, never clear a colony

investigation.where: Cool north slope or hemlock draw where ramps were reported in April
photograph: Leaf cluster from above — no digging
writeDown: Is this colony recovering from prior harvest?
doNotDisturb: Do not harvest on protected land without rules check
Leave flowers for seed if present

scoreNarratives.high: Late-season ramp greens may persist in cool draws — harvest ethically or observe only.
scoreNarratives.moderate: Season fading — study colony health rather than quantity.
scoreNarratives.low: Peak passed — note locations for next April, practice leave-no-trace ethics.

species.name: Blueberries
lookForOutside: Green fruit clusters on sunny south ridges and bog margins
White urn-shaped flowers may still appear on late plants
Birds will compete — observe phenology, harvest only when fully blue

investigation.where: South ridge scrub or plateau bog edge with Vaccinium
observe: Flower vs green fruit vs ripe berry
writeDown: Compare sunny vs shaded plants on same slope
doNotDisturb: Leave fruit for wildlife on most shrubs
Stay on trail in bog margins

scoreNarratives.high: Fruit development on favored sunny edges — ripeness approaching on south aspects.
scoreNarratives.moderate: Flowers and green fruit visible — scout now, harvest later in June.
scoreNarratives.low: Early phenology only — map shrubs and return when berries turn blue.
